one_line_takeaway: SRTJ reframes black-box LLM jailbreaking as accumulation and ASP-based symbolic composition of reusable, hierarchical attack rules (rather than raw prompt search or fine-tuning), reporting 87.875% average HarmBench ASR across four LLMs -- a 40.88% relative gain over the strongest single-turn baseline (CodeAttack) -- while using roughly 6x less memory (tokens) than naive prompt-storage retrieval.
key_finding: On HarmBench single-turn comparison (Table 1), SRTJ achieves the best ASR on every target model and the best average: 94.0% (GPT-3.5), 90.5% (GPT-4o), 75.0% (LLaMA-3-8B), 92.0% (LLaMA-3-70B), average 87.875%, versus the strongest single-turn baseline CodeAttack's average of 62.375% -- a reported 40.88% relative average improvement, with per-model relative gains of 23.68% (GPT-3.5), 30.22% (GPT-4o), 10.29% (LLaMA-3-8B), 39.39% (LLaMA-3-70B). Against multi-turn baselines (Table 2), the single-turn SRTJ beats ActorBreaker (avg 81.875%) on three of four models -- relative gains of 19.75% (GPT-3.5), 7.10% (GPT-4o), 7.60% (LLaMA-3-70B), average 7.33% -- but is 5.06% relatively worse than multi-turn methods on LLaMA-3-8B, where ActorBreaker/Crescendo-style iterative context accumulation wins. Ablation (Fig. 4a, LLaMA-3-8B) shows removing ASP causes a noticeable ASR drop and removing hierarchical memory causes an even larger drop versus full SRTJ, with the paper attributing the memory ablation's larger effect to noisy/non-transferable experience accumulation. Memory efficiency (Table 3, LLaMA-3-8B): naive rule library holds 171 rules at 1.21 MB / 277,601 total tokens (1,623 avg tokens/rule) versus SRTJ's distilled memory of 56 rules at 0.20 MB / 44,789 total tokens (800 avg tokens/rule) -- roughly a 3x reduction in rule count and 6x reduction in total tokens/storage. Round-count analysis shows ASR generally rises from round 1 to 5 with saturation around rounds 4-5 for GPT-3.5/GPT-4o/LLaMA-3-70B, while LLaMA-3-8B shows slower, less stable early gains with a jump at round 4.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SRTJ (Self-Evolving Rule-Driven Training-Free Jailbreak), a black-box, training-free LLM jailbreak framework that (1) models jailbreaking as a sequential decision process over an explicit evolving state X_t = (R_t, E_t) of a rule library and an experience repository, (2) represents distilled attack strategies as symbolic rules rho = (u, Phi, z, omega) (natural-language description, symbolic predicates, semantic tags, usage/success statistics) rather than raw prompts or fine-tuned weights, (3) uses Answer Set Programming (ASP) as a symbolic solver to select and compose compatible, constraint-satisfying rule subsets (applicability, mutual exclusion, coverage, and bans on previously failed rule combinations) given an attack goal, then hands the selected rule set to an LLM (generator G) purely for natural-language prompt realization, and (4) maintains a hierarchical multi-level rule memory (short-, middle-, and long-term rules) that promotes/demotes rules based on usage and success-rate statistics, fed by verifier feedback (a scalar success score and textual failure analysis) that never updates model parameters. Algorithm 1 formalizes the closed-loop per-goal procedure: exploratory 'BlindLoop' generation when the rule library is empty, else ASP-based rule retrieval with retrieval size K narrowed after repeated ASP failures, prompt synthesis, execution against the target model M, verifier scoring, rule/statistics updates, and fallback to BlindLoop after a low-score streak or exhausted budget.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only query access to a target LLM M (no parameters, training data, internal safety policy, or gradients); attacker submits a bounded sequence of prompts under a strict query budget B and receives responses, evaluated by an internal verifier V (not the final scoring judge) that returns a scalar success score s_t and textual failure analysis f_t, with binary success y_t = I(s_t >= tau). The attacker's stated goal is training-free, transferable jailbreak-strategy discovery across different target models, attack goals, and semantic categories, explicitly ruling out gradient-based/fine-tuning attacks (e.g., GCG-style) as a design constraint. Targets evaluated: GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106, GPT-4o, LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, LLaMA-3-70B-Instruct.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include a dedicated limitations section in the extracted pages (may be in an unread appendix). Notable gaps visible from the main text: (1) all baseline numbers in Tables 1-2 are marked '*' as reported from the ActorBreaker paper rather than independently reproduced by the authors, so head-to-head comparability depends on matching experimental conditions the authors did not verify themselves; (2) the automated GPT-4o judge is used as the primary ASR metric, with human-agreement validation deferred to an appendix not captured in the paper; (3) SRTJ underperforms multi-turn interaction on the smaller LLaMA-3-8B model, which the authors attribute to smaller models needing iterative contextual accumulation rather than single-shot rule-composed prompts; (4) the verifier V used for internal feedback during rule harvesting is distinct from the GPT-4o judge used for final scoring, but the paper does not report verifier accuracy/calibration; (5) the method depends on a strong attacker LLM (DeepSeek-R1) for both exploratory generation and prompt realization, and no ablation varying the attacker LLM is shown in the extracted pages.

datasets_used: HarmBench (Mazeika et al., ICML 2024) -- six harm categories: Chemical/Biological, Cybercrime/Intrusion, Harassment/Bullying, Harmful Activities, Illegal Activities, Misinformation/Disinformation
quantitative_results: SRTJ single-turn ASR on HarmBench: GPT-3.5 94.0%, GPT-4o 90.5%, LLaMA-3-8B 75.0%, LLaMA-3-70B 92.0%, average 87.875% (Table 1)
Strongest single-turn baseline CodeAttack average ASR 62.375% vs SRTJ's 87.875% (40.88% relative improvement) (Table 1)
Strongest multi-turn baseline ActorBreaker average ASR 81.875% vs SRTJ's 87.875% (7.33% relative improvement on average, but SRTJ 5.06% relatively worse on LLaMA-3-8B specifically) (Table 2)
Memory efficiency on LLaMA-3-8B (Table 3): naive rule library = 171 rules, 1.21 MB, 277,601 total tokens, 1,623 avg tokens/rule; SRTJ = 56 rules, 0.20 MB, 44,789 total tokens, 800 avg tokens/rule
Ablation on LLaMA-3-8B (Fig. 4a): full SRTJ > SRTJ w/o ASP > SRTJ w/o Hierarchical Memory in ASR (exact percentage values not machine-readable from the radar/bar figure in extracted text)
baselines_compared: Single-turn: GCG, Multilingual, CipherChat, AutoDAN, PAIR, PAP, CodeAttack, ReNeLLM (all results marked '*', reported from the ActorBreaker paper, not re-run)
Multi-turn: CoA, Crescendo, ActorBreaker (all results marked '*', reported from the ActorBreaker paper, not re-run)
